title: Overdrive
summary: Tube-like distortion with a warmer, more musical character than hard clipping. Use on Reese bass for aggression, or pads that need edge without sounding harsh.
steps: Add Overdrive to a bass or synth track that needs more character
Drive knob: 20–40% for warmth and subtle grit. 60–80% for obvious overdrive character
Tone knob: rolls off high-frequency content of the distortion. Lower = darker overdrive. Higher = brighter, more present
Dynamic knob: envelope-following drive — the harder a note is played (higher velocity), the more drive it gets. 0% = constant drive. 50%+ = velocity-responsive grit
Dry/Wet: blend the overdrive with the original signal. 50% is a common starting point — full wet may be too aggressive
Compare with Saturator: Overdrive has more character and 'feel'. Saturator is more precise and controllable. For Reese bass: Overdrive. For sub bass harmonics: Saturator
tips: Reese bass + Overdrive at 40–50% Drive: gives it the gritty, complex character heard in IC and BL3SS tracks
Don't use Overdrive on sub bass as a primary tool — it adds harmonics but less controlled than Saturator. Use Saturator for sub bass, Overdrive for mid-bass (Reese, stab)
The Tone knob is underused. High Tone with high Drive = very bright, harsh. Pull back the Tone to 30–40% when Drive is high to keep it musical
Overdrive at very low Drive (5–10%) with high Dry/Wet (80%) adds subtle tape-like warmth to a bus — similar to Saturator in Analog Clip mode but with a different character
practice_hint: In spinoff-03 or spinoff-05 (both have Reese bass), add Overdrive to the Reese track. Set Drive 40%, Tone 35%. Compare with and without. The Reese should feel more complex and present with Overdrive on.
ableton_version_note: Available in all editions of Live 9+.
